heroOverview: Phthiriasis is an infestation of the skin and coarse body hair by Pthirus pubis, the crab louse — a blood-feeding ectoparasite that has co-evolved with humans for tens of thousands of years. The pubic region is the primary site, but the lice can also colonise axillary, perianal, leg, and chest hair, and occasionally eyebrows and eyelashes. Worldwide prevalence estimates range from 2–10% of the sexually active population, with transmission occurring primarily through skin-to-skin sexual contact. The hallmark symptom is intense itching in the infested area, particularly at night. Phthiriasis is not a marker of poor hygiene — the louse depends on host blood regardless of cleanliness — and it is readily cured with topical insecticidal agents.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 2–10% of the sexually active global population at any point in time; precise surveillance data are limited (WHO, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in sexually active adults aged 15–40; can affect any person with coarse body hair
keyStats.avgAge: Peak incidence in adults aged 18–35, mirroring sexually active population patterns
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated several hundred million episodes annually worldwide, though under-reported due to stigma

definition: Phthiriasis (ICD-10: B85.3) is the infestation of human body hair and skin by Pthirus pubis, the crab louse or pubic louse, a species of sucking louse belonging to the order Phthiraptera. The crab louse is morphologically distinct from the head louse (Pediculus humanus capitis) and body louse (Pediculus humanus corporis): it has a broad, crab-like body approximately 1–2 mm in length, powerful claws adapted for grasping coarse hair shafts widely spaced apart, and a preference for areas with widely spaced terminal hairs — chiefly the pubic region, but also axillary, perianal, abdominal, chest, and thigh hair. Unlike the body louse, Pthirus pubis does not transmit systemic vector-borne diseases in humans. Adult lice feed exclusively on blood, piercing the skin to inject saliva that causes the local allergic response responsible for itching. Females cement oval eggs (nits) to the base of hair shafts, where they hatch in 6–8 days; the complete life cycle from egg to reproductive adult takes approximately 15–17 days. Transmission is almost exclusively through close physical contact, and sexual contact is by far the dominant route among adults. The infestation falls under infectious disease and sexually transmitted infection (STI) services in most healthcare settings, and it is frequently encountered alongside other STIs. Its management requires simultaneous treatment of sexual partners and thorough attention to bedding and clothing.

typesClassification.description: Infestation confined to the pubic and inguinal hair, the most common presentation; causes intense pruritus, bluish macules, and visible nits or lice on examination.
Crab lice colonising the armpit hair; may occur alone or alongside pubic infestation, producing itching and sometimes secondary bacterial infection from scratching.

Rare but clinically significant infestation of the eyelashes or eyebrows, occurring most often in children after contact with an infested adult; produces intense eye irritation, discharge, and lid oedema and requires specialist ophthalmic treatment.

Widespread infestation across multiple body hair sites — pubic, axillary, chest, abdominal, and leg hair — usually in individuals with heavy exposure or delayed diagnosis; more often seen in immunocompromised or heavily exposed patients.

primarySymptoms: Intense pruritus (itching) in the pubic region, typically worst at night, caused by the louse's saliva injected during blood feeding producing a localised allergic hypersensitivity reaction.
Visible nits (eggs) cemented to the shafts of pubic or other coarse body hair close to the skin surface; appear as pale oval specks about 0.5 mm that cannot be easily flicked off the hair shaft.
Visible adult lice — small, brownish, crab-shaped insects about 1–2 mm — seen clinging to hair shafts or moving on the skin surface under careful inspection.

Maculae caeruleae (sky-blue or slate-coloured macules on the lower abdomen, thighs, or flanks) caused by the enzymatic breakdown of haemoglobin by louse saliva; these are highly specific to Pthirus pubis infestation.
Excoriation marks and skin thickening (lichenification) from repeated scratching, which can progress to secondary bacterial infection with localised erythema, crusting, and warmth.
Secondary bacterial infection — folliculitis, impetigo, or local cellulitis — in longstanding cases where scratching has broken the skin barrier.
Irritation and redness of the eyelid margins when the eyelashes are infested (phthiriasis palpebrarum), sometimes with matting of lashes and mucoid discharge.
Rust-coloured or brownish specks in underwear corresponding to louse faeces (frass), which may be noticed before visible lice or nits are found.
Restlessness and difficulty sleeping due to nocturnal itching intensification, particularly in heavily infested individuals.
Lymphadenopathy in the inguinal region in prolonged or heavily scratched infestations where secondary bacterial infection has developed.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild intermittent itching in the pubic area a few days after possible exposure, before visible nits or lice are detectable.
Faint bluish discolouration of small skin areas on the lower abdomen or inner thighs.
Small rust-coloured specks in underwear that are unfamiliar to the person.
Eye irritation or unexplained lid swelling in a child or adult without prior eye symptoms.
emergencySigns: Severe secondary bacterial skin infection with spreading redness, swelling, warmth, and fever, indicating cellulitis requiring antibiotics.
Significant corneal irritation or vision changes in phthiriasis palpebrarum, which needs same-day ophthalmological assessment.
Signs of anaphylaxis or generalised allergic reaction — extremely rare but reported with heavy infestations — requiring emergency care.

causes.cause: Sexual contact with an infested partner
causes.description: Close genital-to-genital or genital-to-body hair skin contact during sexual activity is the dominant transmission route; the louse transfers between hair-bearing skin surfaces when the two people are in prolonged physical proximity for the few seconds needed for transfer.

Prolonged skin-to-skin contact during non-sexual activities — such as shared sleeping between parent and child — can allow lice to transfer, particularly from infested axillary or chest hair to a child's scalp or eyelash region.

Crab lice survive off the host for up to 24–48 hours and can occasionally transfer via shared sheets, towels, or undergarments, though this route is considered much less common than direct contact because the louse cannot jump or fly and prefers the warm skin environment.

causes.cause: Infested eyelashes transmitted to a child
causes.description: In children, phthiriasis palpebrarum most commonly results from contact with an infested adult caregiver; lice or nits transfer to the lashes during close facial contact and should always prompt safeguarding consideration in young children.

causes.cause: Concurrent heavy body hair infestation spreading to secondary sites
causes.description: A primary pubic infestation left untreated can gradually colonise axillary, abdominal, chest, and leg hair as the louse population grows, and — rarely — the eyelashes and eyebrows in severe disseminated cases.

riskFactors.description: Having multiple concurrent or successive sexual partners substantially increases the probability of contact with an infested individual; consistent barrier contraception reduces risk but does not eliminate it because skin-to-skin contact is the transmission mode.

Phthiriasis is frequently diagnosed alongside other sexually transmitted infections; a diagnosis of pubic lice should prompt testing for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, and HIV because of the shared transmission route.
Sexually active adults aged 18–35 represent the highest-incidence group, mirroring the demographics of sexual activity and the social networks that facilitate transmission.
Shared sleeping facilities — hostels, barracks, shelters — increase the probability of contact transmission via bedding, though this is a lesser contributor than sexual contact.

Immunocompromised individuals may develop heavier infestations because the normal inflammatory pruritic response that prompts early treatment is attenuated, leading to later diagnosis and greater louse populations.
Stigma associated with an STI-related diagnosis leads many people to delay seeking care, during which time the louse population expands and the risk of onward transmission to partners continues.

riskFactors.factor: Failure to treat sexual partners simultaneously
riskFactors.description: Treating only the index case without notifying and simultaneously treating all recent sexual partners virtually guarantees re-exposure within days to weeks.

affectedDemographics: Sexually active adults aged 18–35, the peak incidence group
Individuals with multiple recent sexual partners
People diagnosed with concurrent sexually transmitted infections
Children with phthiriasis palpebrarum — eyelash infestation transferred from an infested caregiver
Residents of overcrowded institutional settings with shared bedding
diagnosisOverview: Phthiriasis is diagnosed primarily by clinical examination. The combination of pruritus in the pubic or other coarse-hair regions plus the direct visualisation of nits on hair shafts or lice on the skin surface is sufficient for a confident diagnosis without laboratory testing. A magnifying glass or dermatoscope improves detection sensitivity considerably — nits appear as oval yellowish or white structures firmly attached to the hair shaft and are distinguished from dandruff by their firm adhesion. Adult lice, if motile, may be seen crawling slowly; if recently fed they appear brownish-red. In cases of phthiriasis palpebrarum, the ophthalmologist uses a slit lamp to visualise lice and nits on the eyelashes. The presence of maculae caeruleae (bluish macules) on the abdomen or thighs is a useful clinical sign seen in perhaps 20–40% of infestations. Because phthiriasis is strongly associated with other STIs, diagnosis should routinely prompt a full sexual health screen including tests for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, and blood-borne viruses. In children in whom phthiriasis palpebrarum is found, safeguarding procedures should be followed and the possibility of sexual abuse assessed. No molecular or serological tests are used in routine clinical practice; the diagnosis remains visual.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies visible lice and nits on the hair shafts of infested areas — the primary and usually sufficient diagnostic test.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines the pubic, axillary, and other body hair regions in good light, using a hand lens or dermatoscope to improve visualisation of small nits cemented to hair shafts.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides magnified visualisation of lice and nits attached to hair shafts, improving detection sensitivity and allowing differentiation of nits from hair casts or dandruff.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small handheld device with a light and magnifying lens is held against the skin; the procedure is painless and takes only a few minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when phthiriasis palpebrarum is suspected; allows precise identification of lice and nits on the eyelash shafts.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You sit in front of a specialised ophthalmic microscope; the examination is painless and takes 5–10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Full STI screen (nucleic acid amplification tests for chlamydia and gonorrhoea, syphilis serology, HIV test)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Performed as routine practice because pubic lice and STIs share the same transmission route and frequently co-exist.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A urogenital swab or urine sample for chlamydia and gonorrhoea; a blood draw for syphilis and HIV; results are available within 24–72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Microscopy of plucked hair with attached nit
diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when the diagnosis is uncertain; the nit and hair are examined under a compound microscope to confirm species, distinguishing Pthirus pubis nits from those of Pediculus species.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single hair with an attached nit is removed and placed on a glass slide for laboratory examination; results take hours to days.

treatmentOverview: Phthiriasis is highly treatable with a short course of topical insecticidal agents, and the goal of treatment is complete eradication of both adult lice and viable nits in a single or two-dose regimen. The two most widely used first-line agents are permethrin 1% cream rinse (applied to all infested areas for 10 minutes before rinsing) and malathion 0.5% lotion (applied for 8–12 hours). Both agents kill adult lice and nymphs; because neither reliably kills all eggs at first application, a second treatment 7–9 days later — when any surviving eggs have hatched but before the new nymphs reach reproductive maturity — is standard practice in most guidelines including BASHH and CDC. All sexual partners from the preceding month should be notified and treated simultaneously to prevent reinfection. Bedding, clothing, and towels that have been in contact with the infested person in the past two days should be machine-washed at 50°C or above and dried on a hot cycle. Items that cannot be laundered can be sealed in a plastic bag for two days, beyond the louse's off-host survival time. Oral ivermectin (200 mcg/kg as a single dose, repeated after 7–10 days) is reserved for cases where topical treatment has failed or is impractical, or for phthiriasis palpebrarum, where it avoids the need to apply insecticides near the eye. Phthiriasis palpebrarum is alternatively treated with petrolatum (petroleum jelly) applied to the eyelids twice daily for 10 days to suffocate the lice, combined with mechanical nit removal. Physical shaving of the infested area, while not necessary, can reduce the nit load and improve the efficacy of topical treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Permethrin 1% cream rinse
medicalTreatments.description: A synthetic pyrethroid insecticide applied to all dry infested body hair for 10 minutes and then rinsed off; both doses (at day 0 and day 7–9) are applied to all involved hair-bearing areas, not only the pubic region.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates of approximately 70–80% after a single application; two-dose regimen achieves approximately 90–95% cure.

medicalTreatments.name: Malathion 0.5% aqueous lotion
medicalTreatments.description: An organophosphate agent applied to all infested areas and left for 8–12 hours before washing off; the longer contact time gives superior ovicidal activity compared to permethrin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 85–95% with a two-dose regimen; preferred in some guidelines as first-line for its stronger ovicidal action.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral ivermectin
medicalTreatments.description: Given at 200 mcg/kg orally as a single dose repeated after 7–10 days; used when topical agents have failed, when the infestation is severe or widespread, or as the preferred route in phthiriasis palpebrarum.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series report cure rates of approximately 80–95% with the two-dose regimen; particularly effective for widespread or treatment-resistant infestation.

medicalTreatments.name: Petrolatum (petroleum jelly) for phthiriasis palpebrarum
medicalTreatments.description: Applied thickly to the eyelid margins twice daily for 8–10 days to suffocate lice and nymphs; combined with mechanical removal of nits from lashes using forceps.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in the majority of reported cases when applied consistently; avoids the ocular toxicity risk of applying insecticidal agents near the eye.

alternativeTreatments.name: Wet-combing and mechanical nit removal
alternativeTreatments.description: Fine-toothed nit combing after application of a conditioner or occlusive agent can physically remove lice and nits; used as an adjunct to insecticidal treatment rather than as a standalone therapy for crab lice because the widely spaced coarse hair makes combing less effective than in head lice.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tea-tree oil formulations
alternativeTreatments.description: Some patients use diluted tea-tree oil as a natural pediculicide; laboratory data suggest pediculicidal activity but robust clinical trial evidence in phthiriasis specifically is lacking, so it is not recommended as a substitute for evidence-based topical agents.

whySeeSpecialist: A sexual health specialist or genitourinary medicine clinic should be consulted for any case of pubic lice to ensure simultaneous partner notification, STI co-screening, and correct treatment of all affected body hair regions. Phthiriasis palpebrarum requires ophthalmic specialist assessment. If treatment fails after two correctly applied courses, a specialist can assess for pyrethroid resistance and prescribe alternative agents.
doctorSelectionGuide: A sexual health (GUM) clinic is the optimal first stop because it combines diagnosis, treatment prescription, partner notification services, and a full STI screen. For eyelash infestation, an ophthalmologist is essential. In primary care, a clinician familiar with STI management and experienced in examining for ectoparasites will ensure all body sites are checked and partners are contacted.
preventionStrategies: Limit sexual contact with new partners and use barrier methods, which reduce but do not eliminate crab louse transmission.
Notify all recent sexual partners (within the past 4 weeks) immediately after diagnosis so they can be examined and treated simultaneously.
Wash all bedding, clothing, and towels used in the past two days at 50°C or above, or seal in a plastic bag for 48 hours.
Do not share towels, bedding, or clothing with an infested individual.
Attend a sexual health clinic for a full STI screen when diagnosed, as co-infections are common.
Avoid sexual contact until both you and your partner(s) have completed the full two-dose treatment course and symptoms have resolved.
lifestyleModifications: Complete both doses of topical treatment on the correct schedule (day 0 and day 7–9) even if itching improves after the first application.
Examine all hairy areas of the body — not just the pubic region — and apply treatment everywhere lice or nits are found.
Resist scratching to avoid secondary skin infection; over-the-counter oral antihistamines can help relieve nocturnal itching.
Seek a full sexual health review as part of routine management, not only at the time of diagnosis.
Avoid sexual contact until treatment of both partners is confirmed complete.

recommended: No specific dietary changes are required for phthiriasis management
Maintain adequate protein and micronutrient intake to support skin healing in cases with secondary infection
avoid: No specific dietary restrictions apply to phthiriasis

exerciseGuidelines: No exercise restrictions are required during or after treatment for phthiriasis. Showering promptly after exercise may help remove louse faeces and reduce irritation during active infestation, and good hygiene around sports kit and shared towels is sensible.
dailyManagement: Apply the second dose of insecticidal treatment precisely 7–9 days after the first, even if symptoms appear resolved.
Check all treated areas again after the second treatment for any remaining live lice or hatching nymphs.
Ensure sexual partner(s) apply their own treatment on the same schedule.
Contact the sexual health clinic if symptoms persist after two completed treatment courses.
For phthiriasis palpebrarum, continue petrolatum applications for the full 8–10 days and follow up with the ophthalmologist to confirm eyelash clearance.
prognosis: The prognosis for phthiriasis is excellent with correct treatment. When both the index patient and all recent sexual partners complete a two-dose topical insecticidal regimen and environmental decontamination of bedding and clothing is performed, cure rates approach 90–95%. The main reason for treatment failure is incomplete partner treatment leading to re-infestation, failure to treat all infested body hair regions, or resistance to pyrethroid insecticides in regions where these are heavily used. Phthiriasis palpebrarum responds well to either the petrolatum protocol or oral ivermectin, with complete louse clearance in the great majority of cases. There are no long-term complications once the infestation is eradicated, other than the secondary bacterial skin infections that can arise from prolonged scratching. Recurrence is common if exposure to an untreated or new infested partner continues.
recoveryTimeline: With correct treatment, itching typically begins to diminish within two to three days of the first application as living lice are killed. Full resolution of itching may take seven to ten days because dead lice and nits remain on hair shafts and the allergic skin reaction subsides gradually. After the second treatment dose on day 7–9, no further live lice or hatching nymphs should be present. Maculae caeruleae fade over two to four weeks. Secondary skin infection, if present, resolves within the standard antibiotic treatment period of five to seven days.
complications: Secondary bacterial skin infection (folliculitis, impetigo, or cellulitis) from repeated scratching, requiring topical or systemic antibiotics.
Concurrent sexually transmitted infection — detected at STI co-screening — which may include chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, or HIV.
Ocular damage in phthiriasis palpebrarum: corneal irritation, conjunctivitis, and in severe prolonged cases blepharitis requiring ongoing ophthalmological management.
Recurrent infestation due to re-exposure from an untreated partner or new exposure, particularly if partner notification is incomplete.
Psychological distress and stigma associated with an STI-linked diagnosis, which can delay treatment-seeking and partner notification.

supportResources.description: U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention overview of transmission, diagnosis, and treatment of pubic lice.

supportResources.name: BASHH — UK Guideline for the Management of Pubic Lice
supportResources.description: British Association for Sexual Health and HIV clinical guidelines for diagnosing and treating phthiriasis, including partner management.

sources.title: BASHH. UK National Guideline for the Management of Phthiriasis Pubis (Pubic Lice). British Association for Sexual Health and HIV, 2021

confusedWithConditions.name: Scabies
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scabies is caused by a burrowing mite, not a louse, and produces burrows between fingers, on wrists, and across the body rather than lice visible on hair shafts; scabies lacks the maculae caeruleae specific to phthiriasis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema causes chronic pruritic skin changes without visible parasites, eggs, or the characteristic bluish macules of phthiriasis; the two are distinguished by inspection for lice and nits.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic contact dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Contact dermatitis in the genital area from latex, fragrances, or detergents causes itching and redness without visible lice, nits, or maculae caeruleae.

relatedConditions.name: Scabies
relatedConditions.relevance: Another ectoparasite infestation transmitted primarily through close skin contact, including sexually, and frequently diagnosed alongside phthiriasis in sexual health settings.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
relatedConditions.relevance: An STI that shares the same transmission route as pubic lice and is part of the standard co-infection screen when phthiriasis is diagnosed.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhoea
relatedConditions.relevance: A bacterial STI commonly diagnosed at the same sexual health visit as phthiriasis because of the shared skin-to-skin transmission pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Syphilis
relatedConditions.relevance: Syphilis co-infection is screened for routinely when pubic lice are diagnosed, given the overlapping sexual transmission route.

faqs.question: What are pubic lice?
faqs.answer: Pubic lice (crab lice) are tiny blood-sucking insects called Pthirus pubis that infest coarse body hair, mainly in the pubic region but also armpits, chest, and eyelashes. They are about 1–2 mm wide with claw-shaped legs suited to gripping widely spaced coarse hairs.

faqs.question: How do you get pubic lice?
faqs.answer: Pubic lice spread almost always through skin-to-skin contact during sexual activity. The louse cannot jump or fly — it needs prolonged physical contact to transfer between people. Rarely, shared bedding or clothing can transmit them.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of pubic lice?
faqs.answer: The main symptom is intense itching in the pubic area, particularly at night. You may also see tiny oval nits stuck to hair shafts, small moving lice on the skin, rust-coloured specks in underwear, or bluish-grey spots on the lower abdomen or thighs.

faqs.question: How are pubic lice treated?
faqs.answer: Apply a pediculicide lotion — usually permethrin 1% or malathion 0.5% — to all infested body hair regions and leave it on as directed. A second application 7–9 days later is needed to kill newly hatched lice. Wash all recently used bedding and towels at 50°C or above. Treat all sexual partners at the same time.

faqs.question: Do I need to see a doctor for pubic lice?
faqs.answer: You can treat pubic lice with an over-the-counter pediculicide in many countries, but a sexual health clinic visit is advisable because pubic lice are often accompanied by other sexually transmitted infections. A clinician can also ensure all body sites are treated and partners are properly notified.

faqs.question: Can pubic lice spread to other parts of the body?
faqs.answer: Yes. While the pubic region is the primary site, crab lice can colonise axillary, abdominal, chest, and leg hair, and in rare cases the eyelashes and eyebrows. A full-body inspection should be performed when treatment is applied.

faqs.question: How long does it take to cure pubic lice?
faqs.answer: With a correctly applied two-dose regimen on day 0 and day 7–9, most people are clear of live lice within 10 days. Itching may persist for a further week as the skin reaction subsides. Dead nits remain on hair shafts and can be removed with a fine comb.

faqs.question: Can pubic lice live on furniture or toilet seats?
faqs.answer: Lice survive off the host for at most 24–48 hours and cannot live on hard surfaces like toilet seats because they need the warmth and blood supply of human skin. The risk from toilet seats is negligible. The meaningful environmental reservoir is bedding and clothing used in the past 48 hours.

faqs.question: Do I need to tell my sexual partners?
faqs.answer: Yes — this is essential. All sexual partners from the past four weeks should be examined and treated at the same time as you. Failing to treat partners is the single most common reason for reinfection.

faqs.question: Are pubic lice a sign of poor hygiene?
faqs.answer: No. Pthirus pubis is an obligate blood-feeder that infests any human with coarse body hair regardless of cleanliness. Frequent bathing does not kill lice. It is a common misconception that pubic lice are linked to poor personal hygiene.

faqs.question: Can pubic lice infest a child's eyelashes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Phthiriasis palpebrarum — eyelash infestation — most often occurs in children and is acquired through close non-sexual contact with an infested adult, such as shared sleeping. It must be taken seriously because it can damage the eye and requires a safeguarding assessment to rule out sexual abuse.

faqs.question: What are the blue spots associated with pubic lice?
faqs.answer: Maculae caeruleae are bluish-grey flat marks on the lower abdomen, inner thighs, or flanks caused by the louse's saliva breaking down haemoglobin in the skin. They are harmless, specific to crab lice infestation, and fade over a few weeks after treatment.

faqs.question: Can shaving the pubic area treat pubic lice?
faqs.answer: Shaving can reduce the nit load and makes topical treatment easier but is not sufficient on its own because nits hatch before all hair regrows and lice can remain on remaining shorter hair or nearby body sites. Shaving is an optional adjunct to, not a replacement for, insecticidal treatment.

faqs.question: What if my pubic lice come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence is almost always due to re-exposure from an untreated partner rather than treatment failure. Confirm that all partners were treated on the same schedule. If lice persist after two correct treatment courses, a sexual health clinic can assess for pyrethroid resistance and prescribe oral ivermectin.

faqs.question: What should I do with my bedding when I have pubic lice?
faqs.answer: Machine-wash at 50°C or higher and tumble-dry on a hot setting all bedding, clothing, and towels used in the 48 hours before treatment. Items that cannot be washed should be sealed in a plastic bag for at least 48 hours. Vacuum mattresses and upholstered furniture.

faqs.question: Are pubic lice the same as head lice?
faqs.answer: No. Pubic lice (Pthirus pubis) and head lice (Pediculus humanus capitis) are different species. Crab lice have a broader, wider body suited to widely spaced coarse hair, while head lice have an elongated body for finer, closely spaced scalp hair. The infestations are treated similarly but occur in different body regions.

faqs.question: Can I use head lice treatment for pubic lice?
faqs.answer: Many of the same active agents — such as permethrin and malathion — treat both head lice and pubic lice, but the application area and method differ. For pubic lice, all infested body hair regions must be treated, not only the scalp. Check product labelling and ask a pharmacist or clinician for guidance specific to phthiriasis.

faqs.question: Do I need to be tested for other STIs if I have pubic lice?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pubic lice are transmitted through the same close skin contact as many sexually transmitted infections. A full STI screen — including tests for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, syphilis, and HIV — is recommended at the time of diagnosis. Many sexual health clinics provide this as a package.

faqs.question: Is pubic lice infestation dangerous?
faqs.answer: Phthiriasis is not life-threatening but causes significant discomfort and carries the risk of co-infection with other STIs. The main complications are secondary bacterial skin infection from scratching and, in eyelash infestation, potential eye damage. Both are treatable. The infestation itself poses no risk of serious organ damage.

faqs.question: Can pregnant women use pubic lice treatments?
faqs.answer: Permethrin is generally considered safe in pregnancy and is the preferred first-line agent. Malathion and oral ivermectin should be used with caution and only after discussion with a healthcare provider. Pregnant women should seek clinical guidance rather than self-treating.

metaDescription: Phthiriasis (pubic lice) causes intense itching in the groin from Pthirus pubis infestation. Learn symptoms, topical treatments and partner management.
